title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Lab 13
text:
NoteOpen in Google Colab (R)



Open in Colab — after it opens, use Runtime -> Change runtime type and select R if needed.





By the end of this lab, you should be able to:

Connect a Google Sheet to R using a CSV export link.
Load data in Colab with readr::read_csv() (same pattern as TidyTuesday URLs).
Fit a simple linear regression model with lm(response ~ predictor).
Print and interpret model fit output (summary(), coef()).
Make a point prediction and a confidence interval for the mean response using predict().
Create and interpret a scatterplot with fitted line.





Use class/lab time to make progress on Project 5. This demo gives a complete, minimal workflow:

Google Sheet data collection
loading in R
fitting linear model
prediction + interval
simple plot





If you are running this outside Colab in RStudio:

Script/Source pane (top-left): where you write and save code (.R script or .qmd).
Console (bottom-left): where code runs and output appears.
Terminal tab (bottom-left, next to Console): shell commands (ls, git status, etc.) if needed.
Environment/Files/Plots pane (right): inspect objects, files, and figures.

Typical workflow:

Write code in the script.
Run selected lines to the Console (Ctrl+Enter / Cmd+Enter).
Check plots/results, then save script/notebook.





A practical next step from .ipynb is a .qmd file for a more reproducible workflow (same code + clean rendered output such as PDF/HTML).
Motivation: ultimately, in statistics we usually need to deliver a clear document/PDF/slides that communicates results. Quarto is a strong workflow for this because it keeps a direct connection between your final output, your analysis code, and your dataset. That reproducible link makes your work easier to check, update, and trust.


---
title: "Mini Demo"
format:
  pdf: default
---

# Mini analysis

```r
# Quarto chunk options would normally go at the top of a real chunk, e.g.:
# #| echo: false
# #| fig-cap: "Scatterplot of response vs predictor."
# #| fig-width: 5
# #| fig-height: 3.5
library(tidyverse)
dat <- readr::read_csv("https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/YOUR_SHEET_ID/export?format=csv&gid=0")
ggplot(dat, aes(predictor, response)) + geom_point()
```




YAML (the top --- block): document metadata and output format.
Chunk option lines (#| ...): instructions for rendering (caption, plot size, hide/show code, etc.).
echo: false hides code but still shows output/figure.
Use echo: true when you want students/readers to see code.




From Terminal:
quarto render your-file.qmd --to pdf




Quarto Cheatsheet (Posit)
Quarto Documentation






Create a sheet with a header row, for example:

predictor
response

Then:

Open your Google Sheet.
Click Share -> set access to Anyone with the link (Viewer) for class/demo use.
Copy the spreadsheet ID from the URL:

URL looks like https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/SPREADSHEET_ID/edit#gid=0

Build a CSV export URL:

https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/SPREADSHEET_ID/export?format=csv&gid=0

Use that URL directly in read_csv().

This is the same idea as reading a raw CSV from GitHub/TidyTuesday: read_csv("https://...csv").






Code
if (!requireNamespace("tidyverse", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("tidyverse", repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")
}
library(tidyverse)


Replace sheet_csv_url with your own export link:


Code
# Example placeholder: replace with your actual sheet CSV export URL
sheet_csv_url <- "https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/YOUR_SHEET_ID/export?format=csv&gid=0"

# dat <- read_csv(sheet_csv_url, show_col_types = FALSE)

# For this page preview only, we create a fallback demo dataset:
set.seed(1313)
dat <- tibble(
  predictor = runif(40, 2, 12),
  response = 8 + 2.5 * predictor + rnorm(40, 0, 3)
)

glimpse(dat)


Rows: 40
Columns: 2
$ predictor <dbl> 10.828502, 5.842035, 9.093150, 6.944254, 8.381935, 8.318950,…
$ response  <dbl> 38.34909, 20.65276, 30.32511, 27.19635, 27.59800, 28.90071, …


Code
head(dat, 8)


# A tibble: 8 × 2
  predictor response
      <dbl>    <dbl>
1     10.8      38.3
2      5.84     20.7
3      9.09     30.3
4      6.94     27.2
5      8.38     27.6
6      8.32     28.9
7      4.41     18.4
8      3.00     20.8


Fit simple linear model:


Code
fit <- lm(response ~ predictor, data = dat)
summary(fit)



Call:
lm(formula = response ~ predictor, data = dat)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-6.8535 -1.7948 -0.4836  2.3362  7.2845 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)   5.2368     1.3195   3.969  0.00031 ***
predictor     2.7647     0.1641  16.850  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 2.955 on 38 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.882, Adjusted R-squared:  0.8789 
F-statistic: 283.9 on 1 and 38 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16


Code
coef(fit)


(Intercept)   predictor 
   5.236843    2.764674 


Prediction at a new value:


Code
new_x <- tibble(predictor = 8)

# Point prediction
predict(fit, newdata = new_x)


       1 
27.35424 


Code
# Confidence interval for mean response at predictor = 8
predict(fit, newdata = new_x, interval = "confidence", level = 0.95)


       fit      lwr      upr
1 27.35424 26.39527 28.31321


Scatterplot with fitted line:


Code
ggplot(dat, aes(x = predictor, y = response)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8, color = "steelblue4") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "darkred") +
  labs(
    title = "Project 5 demo: response vs predictor",
    x = "Predictor",
    y = "Response"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()














Team selected final predictor and response columns.
Google Sheet has clean headers and units.
Data loads in Colab via read_csv(sheet_csv_url).
lm() model runs without errors.
Team can report fitted equation and one prediction with confidence interval.
Plot is labeled and ready for one-page summary.






403/permission error: sheet is not shared for viewing.
Wrong sheet tab: gid in URL points to a different tab.
Column name mismatch: check exact header spelling with names(dat).
Non-numeric columns: convert with as.numeric() only after checking raw values/units.





For Project 5, this workflow is intentionally minimal: a clean reproducible path from shared data to interpretable prediction output in R.
